what_it_does: Acceptance test for the inbox triage system: it surfaces decisions in different states, resolves one manually, then verifies that decisions are sorted into live_escalations, resolved_for_you, and batched lanes, that counts are reported, and that the original raw payload remains drillable from a surfaced decision.
observations: Uses a temporary FsStore and discovers nodes from the nodes directory to build a Suite.
Defines a local check() helper that asserts a condition, increments a global PASS counter, and prints ok.
Surfaces two pending code_build decisions with default reject and one auto-proceeded spend decision.
Calls suite.inbox.resolve() to move one pending decision into the resolved lane.
Batching only appears when a theme has two or more pending decisions.
Drillability is checked by reading d['payload']['code'] from the surfaced decision record.
The docstring references slice 6, F1-F2, C2-C3 and context-05.
standouts: Deterministic lane classification is the central property under test.
The drillability assertion explicitly ties the value layer back to the preserved raw payload.
The batch threshold of two pending decisions per theme is directly exercised.
conventions: Acceptance test instantiates real runtime objects (FsStore, NodeRegistry, Suite).
Uses an inline check() helper and a global PASS counter for assertions.
Cleans up the temporary store directory in a finally block.
Discovers nodes from a filesystem path relative to the project root.
concerns: check() relies on a global mutable PASS counter.
Lane names, count keys, and batch threshold are hard-coded against suite.inbox_lanes() output.
No explicit assertion that the temporary store was fully cleaned up.
The test does not document the expected behavior when multiple themes each reach the batch threshold.
notes: A runtime-backed acceptance test that pins inbox lane sorting and raw-payload drillability for chief-of-staff triage.
questions: What is the exact contract of suite.inbox.surface() for default and resolved arguments?
How does suite.inbox_lanes() decide theme grouping and the batch threshold?
What does value-framing mean in the implementation of the decision compiler?
Are the lane names and count keys a stable API or test-only conventions?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose (inbox lanes + COA drillability, slice 6 F1-F2/C2-C3) matches the actual test, which exercises lane classification, counts, batching, and raw-payload access.
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to prove that surfaced decisions are deterministically routed into triage lanes and that the underlying raw decision payload stays reachable, supporting a chief-of-staff style decision compiler.
novelty: It combines explicit batching-threshold behavior with a drillability assertion that the raw payload is preserved under the value layer in a single acceptance test.
contribution: A concrete acceptance test that records the expected inbox lane behavior and raw-payload drillability contract for the chief-of-staff triage feature, which a fused system would keep as a behavioral specification.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test exercising inbox lane classification, operator resolution, theme batching, and raw-payload drillability using a temporary FsStore-backed Suite.
intention_for_embedding: To validate that surfaced decisions sort into deterministic triage lanes while remaining drillable to their original payloads.

symbols.does: Local assertion helper that increments a global PASS counter and prints ok for each passing check.
symbols.description: check(label, cond) asserts the supplied condition, increments the module-level PASS counter, prints an ok message, and raises an AssertionError with the label if the condition is false. It acts as a minimal inline test harness for this acceptance script.
